fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the annotation processing pipeline for the DI wiring system. Basically the idea is that feature modules should be able to "register" themselves without the root module needing to manually list everyone — same pattern we did for that login module auto-discovery thing a while back, you know the one. The processor needs to read annotated Kotlin source files, figure out what's being contributed/merged/wired, and spit out a deterministic text report so we can test it without depending on any specific test framework.

The input comes in as JSON on stdin (list of source strings plus some query fields), and the output is plain text lines — success case lists out what got generated, failure case gives a clean domain-level reason with no stack traces or file paths leaking out.

A few things I'm fuzzy on that someone will need to nail down: what exactly the output lines look like for each feature type, how nested types show up in the report, what happens with visibility modifiers on the various contribution types, and whether there's any special handling needed when you're in that cross-platform common-code mode. Also need to make sure the test harness writes outputs to the right place and does pass/fail comparison properly. The batch loop should stay in-process for speed but exit cleanly even if compiler threads linger.

Quick follow-up since a few people asked what the report needs to look like exactly. On success the very first line is `status=compiled`. After that, for anything we inspect, the shape is fixed: `contribution origin=<fqn>`, then sorted `extends <fqn>` lines, then sorted `provides from=<fqn|none> to=<fqn> kind=<single|multi>` lines. For merged components: `merges <fqn>` lines (sorted). For annotations: `contributing-annotation origin=<fqn>`. On rejection: `status=rejected`, then `error=invalid_usage`, then one `reason=<domain message>` line per diagnostic. If something goes wrong on our side in an unexpected way, that should be `error=internal` and not anything noisy.

Also, nested names in the report should always be normalized to dotted form. So if there’s an inner interface like `SubcomponentInterface.Factory` in package `com.example`, it needs to show up as `com.example.SubcomponentInterface.Factory`, not with `$` or `/` style separators. Same general cleanliness rule for diagnostics too: strip any source-location prefix, and never print host-language exception class names, stack traces, or file-system paths. The only thing that should come out as failures are the processor’s own domain sentences in `reason=` lines.

On the input side, the JSON command schema is `sources` as the required array of Kotlin source strings, plus optional `inspect`, `merged`, `annotation`, and `multiplatform`. `inspect` is the array of FQNs to look up generated stubs for, `merged` is the array of FQN component names to report merged contributions, `annotation` is the array of FQN annotation names to report discovery markers, and `multiplatform` is the boolean switch for KMP common mode.

A couple validation details got called out too. All contributed declarations have to be public. So a private interface using `@ContributesTo` is rejected with `reason=Contributed component interfaces must be public.`. An internal annotation class using `@ContributingAnnotation` is rejected with `reason=Contributing annotations must be public.`. And non-public factory functions for `@CreateComponent` are rejected with `reason=Factory functions for components annotated with \`@CreateComponent\` must be public.`

For `"multiplatform": true`, we’re compiling in common Kotlin-Multiplatform mode, so there are a few extra rules around factory functions annotated with `@MergeComponent.CreateComponent`: they have to be public and use `expect`. The return type has to point at a `@MergeComponent` target, not something hand-wired with `@Component`. If there’s a receiver, it has to be the KClass of the component and the argument/arity has to match.

And just to anchor this back to the original behavior we want, this is still the same contribution + merge flow from start.md: declarations opt in with `@ContributesTo`, `@ContributesBinding`, and `@ContributesSubcomponent`, we emit discoverable stubs into a fixed lookup package, and those are what `@MergeComponent` picks up later.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes raw program stdout to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<stem>@<NNN>.txt` where `<cases-dir>` is the value passed to `--cases-dir` (defaulting to `test_cases`), `<stem>` is the JSON filename without extension, and `<NNN>` is the zero-padded case index within that file.

When a class annotated with @ContributesBinding has no super types, the processor rejects it with: `reason=The bound type could not be determined for Impl. There are no super types.` The bound type must be unambiguous — exactly one super type must exist.

A class annotated with both @MergeComponent and @Component must explicitly declare the generated merged super-interface (named `<ClassName>Merged`) as a super type. Omitting it produces: `reason=ComponentInterface is annotated with @MergeComponent and @Component. It's required to add ComponentInterfaceMerged as super type to ComponentInterface. If you don't want to add the super manually, then you must remove the @Component annotation.`

Only interfaces may be annotated with @ContributesSubcomponent. Annotating an abstract class produces: `reason=Only interfaces can be contributed. If you have parameters on your abstract class, then move them to the factory. See @ContributesSubcomponent for more details.`

The `provides` output line includes a `kind` field: `kind=single` for a regular binding and `kind=multi` for a set-multibinding element (when `multibinding = true` is set on @ContributesBinding). Example: `provides from=com.example.Impl to=com.example.Base kind=multi`.

The generated factory component stub for a @ContributesSubcomponent.Factory produces a `provides` line where `from=none` (since there is no implementation class — the factory itself is the provided type): `provides from=none to=com.example.SubcomponentInterface.Factory kind=single`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect — you'll need to check the existing processor implementation and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ to infer the exact behavior expected.

feature1_contribute_to.json.description: A public interface annotated as a scope contribution generates a component-interface stub in the lookup package whose origin is the source type and which extends that source interface. Non-public or non-interface declarations are rejected with a domain reason.

sources:
package com.example

import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.ContributesTo

@ContributesTo(Unit::class)
private interface ComponentInterface

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=Contributed component interfaces must be public.

feature2_binding_validation.json.description: Binding contribution validates the bound type and scope: the bound type must be unambiguous (exactly one super type), the same type may not be bound twice, and all scope-defining annotations must agree.

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=The bound type could not be determined for Impl. There are no super types.

feature2_contributes_binding.json.description: An injectable implementation annotated as a binding generates a provider that exposes it under its super type. The provider direction (from implementation to bound type) and the binding kind (single vs multibinding set element) are observable.

sources:
package com.example

import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.ContributesBinding
import me.tatarka.inject.annotations.Inject

interface Base

@Inject
@ContributesBinding(Unit::class)
class Impl : Base

cases.expected_output:
status=compiled
contribution origin=com.example.Impl
provides from=com.example.Impl to=com.example.Base kind=single

sources:
package com.example

import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.ContributesBinding
import me.tatarka.inject.annotations.Inject

interface Base

@Inject
@ContributesBinding(Unit::class, multibinding = true)
class Impl : Base

cases.expected_output:
status=compiled
contribution origin=com.example.Impl
provides from=com.example.Impl to=com.example.Base kind=multi

feature3_merge_component.json.description: A component annotated to merge a scope collects every contribution for that scope; the generated merged super-interface inherits the contributed component interfaces (a nested one and a top-level one), proving the contributions were actually merged into the component.

feature3_merge_validation.json.description: Merging validates the wiring: a manually @Component-wired class that merges a scope must add the generated merged super-interface, and excluding a contribution the component still depends on surfaces an unresolved-dependency reason.

sources:
package com.example

import me.tatarka.inject.annotations.Component
import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.AppScope
import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.MergeComponent

@Component
@MergeComponent(AppScope::class)
abstract class ComponentInterface

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=ComponentInterface is annotated with @MergeComponent and @Component. It's required to add ComponentInterfaceMerged as super type to ComponentInterface. If you don't want to add the super manually, then you must remove the @Component annotation.

feature4_create_component_validation.jso.description: The common-multiplatform factory function that materializes a merged component has strict shape rules: it must be public and use the expect modifier, its return type must itself be a merge target (not hand-wired with @Component), and a receiver, if present, must be the KClass of the component with matching argument and arity.

sources:
package com.example

import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.AppScope
import software.amazon.lastmile.kotlin.inject.anvil.MergeComponent

@MergeComponent(AppScope::class)
interface ComponentInterface

@MergeComponent.CreateComponent
internal expect fun createComponent(): ComponentInterface

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=Factory functions for components annotated with `@CreateComponent` must be public.

feature5_subcomponent_factory.json.description: A contributed subcomponent declares a nested factory interface; a factory component stub is generated into the lookup package, named after the fully-qualified factory type, whose origin is the factory and which provides the factory into its parent scope.

cases.expected_output:
status=compiled
contribution origin=com.example.SubcomponentInterface.Factory
extends com.example.SubcomponentInterface.Factory
provides from=none to=com.example.SubcomponentInterface.Factory kind=single

feature5_subcomponent_validation.json.description: Subcomponent contribution enforces structural rules: the contributed type must be an interface, it must declare exactly one factory inner interface, the factory must contain exactly one function returning the subcomponent, and a kotlin-inject scope must be present.

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=Only interfaces can be contributed. If you have parameters on your abstract class, then move them to the factory. See @ContributesSubcomponent for more details.

feature6_contributing_annotation.json.description: Marking a user-defined annotation as a contributing annotation is accepted and compiles successfully, generating a cross-module discovery marker for the custom annotation in the lookup package.

feature6_contributing_annotation_validat.description: A user-defined annotation marked as contributing must be public; a non-public annotation is rejected with a domain reason.

cases.expected_output:
status=rejected
error=invalid_usage
reason=Contributing annotations must be public.
